_README: MOCK DATA for the /training course player. Structure: company/sessions/tracks/roster + modules + lessons. Each lesson has a `blocks` array (heading | text | list | callout | prompt | video | context-compare | context-file | context-file-test | prompt-builder). trackId 'all' = shown to everyone; otherwise the lesson only shows for that track (used for role-tailored exercises). Interactive blocks call the Fieldwork Worker (lib/fw-live.ts); they fall back to canned output when NEXT_PUBLIC_FW_WORKER is unset. Facilitator-led exercises (icebreaker, word cloud, policy co-creation) live in /training/facilitator, not here. To go live on a Google Sheet: store sessions/tracks/roster/modules as tabs, and lesson blocks as a markdown column converted on read. See lib/training.ts.

company.name: Rafanelli & Nahas
company.tagline: AI Literacy Training · built with Fieldwork AI

sessions.title: Day 1 — Foundations
sessions.subtitle: Context, prompting, and responsible use — hands-on, in real time.

sessions.title: Session 2 — Applied Workflows & Governance
sessions.subtitle: Turn fluency into real R&N workflows you can trust and reuse.

blocks.scenario: A valued tenant emailed, frustrated that visitor parking keeps filling up and her clients can't find spots. You need a reply that takes it seriously and keeps her happy.
blocks.barePrompt: Write a reply to a tenant who is upset about parking at their building.
blocks.contextBlock: Context: I'm a property manager at Rafanelli & Nahas, a Boise CRE owner/operator whose north star is tenant service. Our voice is warm but firm — never defensive. The tenant is Dana at Suite 210, Boise Plaza, with us 4 years and normally very happy. She emailed frustrated that visitor parking has been full lately and her clients can't find spots. I can't add spaces, but I can look into reserved visitor stalls, a validation arrangement with the garage next door, and clearer signage. I want a reply that takes it seriously, owns it, offers concrete next steps and a timeline, and reassures her we value her — without over-promising.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate property manager. Be concise and match a warm-but-firm professional tone.
blocks.closer: Same model, same ask — the only thing that changed is what it knew. (Dana's invented, but your real tenant work belongs here — that's exactly what our protected plan is for. More on that this afternoon.)

blocks.scenario: A tenant's been complaining about a rattle from the ceiling. You found it — a VAV box above their suite, actuator on its way out. The fix is in motion. Now comes the part nobody loves: writing it up.
blocks.barePrompt: Write up a work order for an HVAC problem in an office building.
blocks.contextBlock: Context: I'm a building engineer at Rafanelli & Nahas, a Boise Class-A office owner/operator — tenant service is the north star here. Here's my field note, exactly how I'd say it out loud: VAV box above suite 410 is rattling, tenant's complained twice this week, popped the ceiling tile — actuator's worn and there's play in the damper linkage, I snugged the linkage so it's quiet for now, but that actuator needs replacing, want our mechanical vendor out this week, tenant's an accounting firm on calls all day so noise is a big deal, and it's the second noise complaint on that floor this month. I need the work order to cover location, symptom, cause, temp fix, parts needed, and priority — plus a two-line update the property manager can send the tenant today.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial building maintenance engineer. Be practical and safety-first.
blocks.closer: Same model, same ask. It didn't tell you what's wrong with the unit — you knew that. It did the write-up.

blocks.intro: Build R&N's shared context file. Fill in what you know — don't worry about polish, and use the mic to talk instead of type.

sections.label: Who we are
sections.hint: Company, history, size — a Boise CRE owner/operator with ~1M sq ft of mostly Class-A office, downtown and at Parkcenter.

sections.label: What we do
sections.hint: Property types, our buildings, the kind of work we do day to day.

sections.label: What makes us different
sections.hint: How we go to market, what we're known for. (Scott's tenant-service ethos goes here.)

sections.label: Our culture & voice
sections.hint: Values, how we treat tenants and each other, how we write.

sections.label: What we run day to day
sections.hint: The buildings (Boise Plaza, Capitol & Idaho, River Quarry…), the systems and equipment you maintain, the vendors you count on. (Facilities — this one's yours.)

sections.label: What AI should always know
sections.hint: Terminology, tone guardrails, things it should never assume.

blocks.guidance: Write a real prompt — a tenant email, a summary, a note, anything from your week. Then run it both ways.
blocks.starterPrompt: Draft a short, friendly note letting a tenant know we'll be doing planned maintenance in their building next week.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate team. Be concise and practical.
blocks.sampleContext: About us: Rafanelli & Nahas is a privately held Boise commercial real estate owner/operator — we own and manage ~1M sq ft of mostly Class-A office downtown and at Parkcenter, plus a hotel and some industrial. Our north star is tenant service (Scott's '10 Commandments of Tenant Service'). We're warm but firm, never adversarial, and we compete on service and relationships, not price. Write like a buttoned-up property team that genuinely cares about its tenants.

blocks.text: More is more. Set it up in three moves — the third is telling Claude to interview you — then answer its questions and push back until it's right.

challenges.label: The late-CAM nudge
challenges.text: Boise Dental Group is 15 days past due on their $4,200 CAM reconciliation balance. Nudge them to commit to a payment date this week — without straining a 6-year relationship.

hints.c1: e.g. a warm-but-firm email that gets a specific payment date this week
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a seasoned property manager; Boise Dental's been with us 6 years and the relationship matters more than the $4,200

challenges.label: Rent-increase talking points
challenges.text: A good tenant's renewal is coming up and you need to raise their rent about 8%. Get talking points that frame it around the value they get, plus a one-line opener for the conversation.

hints.c1: e.g. 3–4 talking points that make the increase feel fair, plus a warm one-line opener
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a seasoned property manager; this is a great long-term tenant I really want to keep

challenges.label: Draft custom lease language
challenges.text: A growing startup tenant wants a 3-year lease but the right to terminate at year 2 if they outgrow the space and we can't relocate them to something bigger. Draft the clause (for your attorney to review).

hints.c1: e.g. clean, plain-English clause language my attorney can review and drop into the lease
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a careful commercial real estate attorney; flag anything that exposes us

challenges.label: The 7:40 a.m. voicemail
challenges.text: A tenant left this overnight: 'Hi, it's Denise on 4 — the women's restroom sink won't stop dripping and it's getting worse. Also the stairwell light by the parking side is out again. Oh, and can someone call me back about two more key fobs?' Turn one rambling message into what you actually need.

hints.c1: e.g. three clean tickets — priority, location, who handles each — plus a friendly callback text so Denise knows we're on it
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a CRE property coordinator; tenant service is our north star — nothing falls through the cracks

chunks.label: Describe the end state
chunks.instruction: What are you trying to accomplish? What does a great result look like?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. a warm-but-firm email that gets a payment date this week

chunks.label: Add the backstory + who Claude should be
chunks.instruction: What's the situation, and who should Claude act as — a seasoned PM? a careful attorney?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Act as a seasoned property manager; tenant service is our north star

chunks.label: Have Claude interview you first
chunks.instruction: The move most people skip — we've pre-filled it so you see it. Keep it or make it your own.
chunks.default: Before you draft, interview me: ask me 2–3 quick questions to fill in anything you're missing.

blocks.interviewInstruction: This is a hands-on practice exercise — never ask me to paste a document or wait on one. If you need details I haven't given you, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and keep going.
blocks.genericPrompt: Write me an email to a tenant.
blocks.genericOutput:
Dear Tenant,

We are writing to inform you regarding your account. Please contact our office at your earliest convenience to discuss. Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,
Management
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate property manager, matching a warm-but-firm professional tone. When the user asks you to interview them first, ask a few concise clarifying questions and wait for answers before drafting. When they push back, revise directly and show the improved version.

blocks.text: You know these buildings better than anyone — this is about making Claude prove it can keep up. Three moves; the third makes Claude interview YOU. Answer its questions, then push back until it's right.
🎤 Not a fast typer? Tap the mic in any box and just talk — Claude fills it in for you. Out in the field, this is how you'll use it most.

challenges.label: Upper-floor pressure mystery
challenges.text: Tenants on the top floors say the water pressure comes and goes. You suspect the booster system. Get a ranked list of likely causes and what to check yourself before you call the plumber.

hints.c1: e.g. ranked likely causes + the checks I can do myself first — I want to walk in knowing more than the plumber expects
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a master plumber who's seen every booster setup; I know this building cold — I'll fill in what you need

challenges.label: Describe it → work order
challenges.text: You just walked a unit and found a problem. Describe what you saw and have Claude turn it into a clean, vendor-ready work order.

hints.c1: e.g. a clean, vendor-ready work order with location, priority, and next steps
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a senior building engineer; here's what I saw on the walk-through…

challenges.label: Vendor quote request
challenges.text: You need a quote to replace a failing condenser fan motor. Draft the request to the vendor with the specs and details they'll need to price it.

hints.c1: e.g. a clear quote request with the specs and details the vendor needs to price it accurately
hints.c2: e.g. Act as a facilities manager getting competitive quotes; be specific, no back-and-forth

chunks.label: Describe the end state
chunks.instruction: What are you trying to accomplish? What does a great result look like?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. a clean work-order entry with priority and next steps

chunks.label: Add the backstory + who Claude should be
chunks.instruction: What's the situation, what have you tried, and who should Claude act as — a senior building engineer?
chunks.placeholder: e.g. Act as a senior building engineer; filter's clean, I don't do refrigerant work

chunks.label: Have Claude interview you first
chunks.instruction: The move most people skip — we've pre-filled it so you see it. Keep it or make it your own.
chunks.default: Before you answer, interview me: ask me 2–3 quick questions about the unit and what I've tried.

blocks.interviewInstruction: This is a hands-on practice exercise — never ask me to paste a document or wait on one. If you need details I haven't given you, make a reasonable assumption, note it, and keep going.
blocks.genericPrompt: How do I fix an AC unit?
blocks.genericOutput: There are many possible reasons an AC unit may not work. Check the power, the thermostat, the filter, and the circuit breaker. If it still doesn't work, you may need to call a professional HVAC technician to inspect it.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial building maintenance engineer — practical and safety-first. When the user asks you to interview them first, ask a few concise clarifying questions and wait for answers before answering. When they push back, revise directly and show the improved version.

lessons.title: Now push back — iterate on real work

blocks.intro: Bring a real task from your week — a tenant email, a summary, a note, a plan. Get a first draft, then push back once and watch it get sharper.
blocks.system: You are assisting a commercial real estate team at Rafanelli & Nahas (a Boise owner/operator; tenant service is the north star). Be concise, practical, and match a warm, professional tone.

blocks.title: The essentials
items: Your data stays yours — on our plan, what you enter isn't used to train models and doesn't leave our environment.
Two tools, two lanes — Claude for company work (context, drafts, analysis); Copilot for what's built into Microsoft 365. Nothing company or client goes anywhere else.
Humans own the output — AI drafts; you decide. Your name, your judgment. "The AI did it" isn't a thing.
Verify what matters — dollar amounts, dates, clause language, legal terms. Trust it on low-stakes drafting.
Know the access boundary — AI can read and draft, but never sends or edits our system of record on its own.
Be transparent — if a client asks "do you use AI?", you should like your honest answer.

blocks.intro: This is our shared agreement — written together, out loud. Chris walks it section by section; you tap the choices that fit on your own screen (that's your take — Chris is assembling the room's copy up front). Anything you'd change or can't settle: Raise it and it lands on the big screen.
blocks.docSubtitle: Working draft — written together, adopted in Session 2

fills.after: — with a human always accountable for the result.
options: free our team for higher-value work
cut the busywork so we focus on judgment and relationships

sections.title: What we use AI for
sections.lead: The everyday work we're comfortable using AI for. Untick any that don't fit; add your own.
items: Drafting emails, letters, and notices
Summarizing leases, long threads, and reports
Researching comps, prospects, specs, and suppliers
Turning rough notes into clean work orders
First-pass diagnostics and troubleshooting
Organizing and cleaning up data
Brainstorming and thinking options through

sections.title: What stays human
sections.lead: AI can help, but a person always finishes and decides these.
items: Final legal review
Signed lease terms and negotiation
Anything a tenant or client sees — before a human reads it
High-stakes financial calls
Hiring, firing, and personnel decisions
Any promise or commitment made on the company's behalf

fills.before: Before anything leaves the building, we verify the

sections.title: How we stay accountable
sections.lead: Humans own every output — our name, our judgment. "The AI did it" isn't a thing here.

fills.before: When something's unsure or goes sideways, we
options: flag it to our point person
stop and check with a human
treat it as a learning, not a blame

fills.before: When AI has materially shaped client-facing work, we
options: are transparent about it if asked

sections.title: Our data rules
sections.lead: Where work happens, and the hard lines.
lines: Claude is where company work happens — our context file, drafts, analysis, and company memory live there.
Copilot stays for what it's built into — email polish, meeting recaps, Excel help inside Microsoft 365.
Outside those two lanes: nothing. No company or client info in free ChatGPT, Gemini, or whatever launches next month.

The everyday work belongs here. Leases, rent schedules, tenant names, budgets, vendor quotes — that's exactly what our protected plan is for. Nothing we enter trains the model or leaves our environment.

Be thoughtful with the truly personal. SSNs, bank and routing numbers, passwords, personal medical or HR details — leave them out unless a task genuinely needs them (a draft almost never does). Rule of thumb: give it what the job needs, nothing more.
AI reads and drafts; it never sends, signs, or edits our system of record on its own.

sections.title: Open questions to settle
sections.lead: Anything we can't settle in the room gets parked here — out loud.
lines: Say it, or use Raise it → below — every open question goes up on the big screen.
Fieldwork, Scott, and Justin resolve them before Session 2 — where the team ratifies the final policy.

blocks.ratify: We'll settle the open questions with leadership and formally adopt this in Session 2.

blocks.title: Two apps, one brain
blocks.text: Claude on your phone = quick answers anywhere (and a camera). Cowork on your desktop = real work over your files. Same brain, same memory of R&N — different doors.

blocks.intro: Not a feature tour — a set of plays. Each one takes minutes, teaches you something real, and ends with proof it worked. We do Tier 1 together in the room; the rest is your quest before Day 2. Your progress shows on the big screen so we can come help — and it picks up on any device.

steps.label: Install the Claude app on your phone
Sign in on both with your R&N account

missions.tier: 1 · Before you leave the room (5 min)
missions.label: Set your profile
missions.do: Open Settings → Profile. Add your name and a line about your role and how you like responses (e.g. “short, lead with the answer, plain language”).
missions.worked: Your next answers come back in that style.
missions.why: Every answer from now on is shaped to your role — 30 seconds that pays forever.

missions.tier: 1 · Before you leave the room (5 min)
missions.label: Set your default model
missions.do: In Settings (or the model menu), set Sonnet as your everyday default — and note where to switch it.
missions.why: Sonnet is the fast daily driver; knowing where the switch lives means you're never stuck.

missions.tier: 1 · Before you leave the room (5 min)
missions.label: Check your data settings
missions.do: In Settings, find the privacy / data controls and read the line about your inputs not being used to train models.
missions.worked: You've seen, in writing, that your data stays yours.
missions.why: You've now seen the answer to “is our stuff safe?” with your own eyes — that's the line to quote when someone asks.

missions.tier: 1 · Before you leave the room (5 min)
missions.label: Turn on voice
missions.do: On your phone, open a chat, tap the mic, allow access, and just say a sentence.
missions.worked: Your words show up as text.
missions.stuck: No mic prompt? Check the app's permissions in your phone settings.
missions.why: In the field or between meetings, talking beats typing every time.

missions.label: Chat vs. Cowork
missions.do: Run the same ask once in Chat, once in Cowork. Chat = quick Q&A. Cowork = it works alongside your files and projects.
missions.worked: You can say, in a sentence, when you'd reach for each.
missions.why: Chat answers questions; Cowork does work over your files. Knowing which door to walk through is half the skill.

missions.label: Switch models
missions.do: In a chat, change the model and ask the same question again.
missions.worked: You've now tried two models on the same task.
missions.why: You'll feel the trade — speed vs. depth — and know when each is worth it.

missions.label: One chat remembers
missions.do: Ask a follow-up in the same chat without repeating yourself — it keeps context. Then open a New chat and notice it's forgotten.
missions.worked: You feel the difference between continuing and starting fresh.
missions.why: That's the context window from today, felt: one thread remembers, a new chat starts blank.

missions.label: Catch it making something up
missions.do: Ask Claude something about your building it can't possibly know — “what year was our roof last recoated?” or “which suite did our biggest tenant start in?” Watch what it does.
missions.why: Either it admits it doesn't know — or it guesses confidently. Seeing that once is why we verify dollar amounts, dates, and clause language.
missions.worked: You caught it (or caught it being honest) — you'll recognize the difference forever.

missions.label: Run the 3 moves on something real
missions.do: Pick a real task from your week and run today's moves in a fresh chat: describe the end state → give the backstory + who Claude should be → tell it to interview you first. Answer its questions, then push back twice.
missions.worked: The draft comes back close to usable — before you even edited.
missions.why: The interview move is the one almost nobody uses — it's also the one that makes the first draft land close.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Create your R&N Project
missions.do: Open the shared R&N Project Justin set up (leasing, tenant relationships, facilities…) — or create your own Project named “R&N” — and paste today's company context file into its instructions.
missions.worked: Chats there already know who R&N is.
missions.why: Paste the context file once, and every chat in that project starts already knowing R&N.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Put it to work
missions.do: In the R&N project: draft a short tenant-newsletter blurb about something real happening at your building — in our voice. Or attach a lease and ask for the key dates, options, and anything unusual.
missions.worked: It sounds like us and gets the specifics right.
missions.why: This is the moment it stops being a demo and starts being your Tuesday.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Put it to work
missions.do: In your R&N project, dictate a messy field note and ask for a clean work order — or describe a symptom and ask for a first-pass diagnostic.
missions.worked: You get a structured, usable result.
missions.why: Messy in, clean out — that's the daily win.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Teach it your voice
missions.do: Paste one email you actually sent that sounds like you. Then: “Write a new draft to [someone] about [topic] — match the voice and tone of my example.”
missions.why: The difference between generic-AI writing and YOUR writing is one example. (This is Justin's daily email habit, generalized.)
missions.worked: It sounds like you, not a robot.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Send Claude a photo
missions.do: You saw it in the room — now on your building: snap an equipment tag or nameplate in your mechanical room and ask Claude to ID the unit and where to buy a replacement part. Nothing to shoot right now? Use the sample tag below.
missions.worked: It reads the model/serial off the plate and returns the part plus suppliers.
missions.stuck: Snap anything with a label — a water heater, a thermostat, even the copier.
missions.why: The manual that talks back — and it finds the suppliers you didn't know you had.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: The everyday habit
missions.do: Your next real email longer than three sentences: draft it in Claude first, tweak, send. Time yourself.
missions.why: Fluency comes from reps on real work, not exercises. This is the rep that becomes automatic.
missions.worked: It's faster by Friday — and automatic by Day 2.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Connect Outlook
missions.do: Settings → Connectors → Outlook → sign in with your R&N account → Allow. Then ask: “what are my 3 most recent emails?”
missions.worked: It lists your actual recent emails.
missions.stuck: Don't see Outlook to connect? IT is still switching this on — flag Justin and skip for now. (Reminder: Claude drafts, it never sends for you.)
missions.why: Once IT flips it on, Claude works over your real inbox — this is where it gets personal.

missions.tier: 3 · This week — your first real win
missions.label: Connect SharePoint
missions.do: Settings → Connectors → SharePoint / OneDrive → sign in → Allow (read-only). Then ask it to find a document by name.
missions.worked: It finds a real file from your drive.
missions.stuck: Not enabled yet? IT's still on it — flag Justin; meanwhile you can just attach files by hand.
missions.why: Your documents become something Claude can find and read — the foundation for the lease skills.

cheatsheet.a: Describe the end state → give backstory + who Claude should be → make it interview you first. Then push back — twice.
Chat = quick questions. Cowork = real work with your files and projects. It's a toggle near the top.
The mic icon in the message box — tap and talk. Best on mobile out in the field.
Tap the model name near the message box. Sonnet is the fast, everyday default.

cheatsheet.q: How do I give it context?
cheatsheet.a: Create a Project and paste your company context file into its instructions once — it's reused in every chat there.
It lets Claude securely see one of your real systems — your Outlook email/calendar, your SharePoint files — so it works with your actual stuff.
On our plan, what you enter isn't used to train models. Connectors are read-only where it matters, and Claude drafts — it never sends for you.
Find four things: New chat, Projects, Artifacts, Settings. Point at those and you know the app.

lessons.title: Recap & your cheat sheet in action

blocks.text: Welcome back. Quick recap of Session 1, then we get to work building real R&N workflows.

items: Your company cheat sheet gives AI context — keep it handy and paste it at the start of serious work.
AI is strong on language and structure, weaker on facts you haven't given it — verify those.
The 4D loop: Delegate the right task, Describe it clearly, Discern what comes back, take Diligence for the result.

blocks.title: Warm-up (5 min)
blocks.text: Pull up your cheat sheet from Session 1 and read it with fresh eyes. Update anything that's changed or that you'd now add. We'll use it throughout today.
Before building anything, find the right thing to build. The best first workflow is repetitive, frequent, fairly standardized, and low-stakes if it's slightly off.

blocks.title: Exercise 1 — audit, categorize, prioritize (20 min)
items: Audit: list 5–10 tasks from your past week that felt repetitive or time-consuming. For each, note how often it happens, how long it takes, how standardized it is, and the cost if done imperfectly.
Categorize: sort them into AI can handle / AI assists, human decides / Human should handle.
Prioritize: pick the one task from the first two buckets that would save you the most time, and write one sentence on why it's your top pick.

blocks.text: Examples by role — Ops: drafting tenant notices, abstracting leases, prepping critical-date lists. Leadership: recurring partner/IC updates, first-pass market scans. Maintenance: work-order write-ups, vendor request emails, bid comparisons.
Take the task you prioritized and describe it precisely using the three parts of Description — Product, Process, Performance. This is the blueprint AI executes against.

items: Product: the end result, the inputs it receives, and the outputs it should produce.
Process: the step-by-step logic, the decision points, when to escalate to a human, and what documents it needs (FAQ, price list, cheat sheet).
Performance: the tone, how to handle uncertainty ("if unsure, flag — never guess"), and the hard nevers.

blocks.title: Exercise 2 — write it, then test it (25 min)
items: Write your Product / Process / Performance descriptions (2–3 sentences each).
Share them with AI along with 3–5 real R&N examples from your work.
Evaluate: did it categorize right? Accurate and on-tone? Did it fabricate anything?
Refine. Plan on 2–3 rounds before it feels reliable — that's normal.

blocks.title: Test-run scaffold
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here is an automation I want to build.
PRODUCT: [what you want back, inputs, outputs]
PROCESS: [steps, decision points, when to escalate]
PERFORMANCE: [tone, how to handle uncertainty, the nevers]

Here are 3–5 real examples to run it on:
[paste examples]

Run each example, then tell me where my description needs to be sharper.
Before you rely on a workflow, think through what could go wrong and how you'll catch it. Diligence is what makes automation safe to trust.

blocks.title: Exercise 3 — three kinds of diligence (20 min)
items: Creation: why is this task a good fit? List 3 failure modes (e.g. wrong pricing, templated reply to a complaint, mixing up tenants) and a safeguard for each.
Deployment: will you review every output or sample? When would you reduce oversight, and what evidence would you need? What would make you pause it entirely?
Transparency: who needs to know AI is involved, and how will you tell them?

blocks.title: Automation Readiness Statement
blocks.text: Wrap it up in 3–5 sentences: what you're automating, why it's appropriate, the safeguards in place, and how you'll be transparent. Keep it as a reference for you and your team.
A short, honest AI policy scales your judgment — so the whole team works from the same understanding instead of making it up case by case.

blocks.title: Exercise — write a one-page policy (20 min)
items: Reflect: What tasks are you now confident handing to AI? What will you always keep human? What do tenants and your team need to know?
Build it with AI: share your cheat sheet and those answers, and ask for a one-page policy with three sections — What we use AI for / What stays human / How we stay accountable.
Stress-test: if a tenant asked "do you use AI in your business?", does this give you a clear, honest answer? Fix anything you softened.

blocks.title: Draft the policy
blocks.text:
[paste cheat sheet]
Here's how I think about AI in our work:
- Confident to hand off: [list]
- Always human: [list]
- What people should know: [list]

Draft a one-page AI use policy for R&N with three sections: "What we use AI for" (approved tasks/tools), "What stays human" (and why), and "How we stay accountable" (oversight, transparency, what happens when something goes wrong). Plain language that sounds like us.

lessons.title: Staying the human in the loop

blocks.text: The point of all this isn't to remove people — it's to free you for the work only people can do.

blocks.title: Key takeaways
items: Being the human in the loop is more than oversight — you decide what problems AI should even solve.
Avoid dependency through understanding, not avoidance: if you can explain what the AI is doing, that's a good sign.
AI should free you for more human work, not less — the personal outreach, the relationship, the judgment call.
Decide as a team what happens with the time AI saves. Set that norm early.
Your AI policy is how this understanding scales as R&N grows.

lessons.title: Your task this week & what's next

blocks.text: Fluency comes from reps. Before you leave, commit to one real task you'll tackle with AI this week — your practice ground.

blocks.title: Final assignment
items: Pick one concrete task waiting for you (a draft, a market scan, a data summary, an onboarding doc, a plan).
Run the full 4D loop on it: Delegation → Description → Discernment → Diligence.
Afterward, jot down what worked, what needed correction, and which competency needs more practice.
Keep a running doc of the prompts and workflows that work for you — so you're not starting from scratch, and teammates can pick them up.
Blueprints: step-by-step guides for common tasks at claude.com/resources/use-cases.
Stay current: rules around AI in business are evolving (FTC guidance, state privacy laws). Revisit these principles as things change.
Questions between sessions? gabe@getfieldworkai.com — that's what office hours are for.

blocks.title: You're done.
blocks.text: You've built a cheat sheet, learned the 4D Framework, mapped your opportunities, built and stress-tested a workflow, and written your AI policy. That's AI fluency. Now go get the reps.
